lines.text: Folks. It's midnight. It's {{CLOCK}} and you know what that means — it means we're five hours deep into this thing and you're still breathing, which puts you ahead of about eleven percent of Madison if Tucker's numbers are right.
And Tucker's numbers are always right. That's the problem with Tucker. You can't argue with someone who's always right. It's like arguing with a calculator.
I'm Nick. You know who I am. You know what I do. And tonight I play records.

This first one goes out to everyone on the East Side who's got their lights off and their doors locked and a baseball bat they've never used for baseball. This is the Ramones. "I Wanna Be Sedated." Yeah. Me too.
Beautiful. You know, Joey Ramone was six-foot-six. Six-foot-six and he looked like he weighed about a hundred and thirty pounds. A human guitar string.
I think about that sometimes. A man that tall choosing to hunch over a microphone in a leather jacket in clubs that smelled like piss. That's commitment. That's vocation.
Most people never find their thing. Joey found his thing at like nineteen and did it until he died. I respect that more than I respect almost anything.
Speaking of things I respect — let me talk about something for a second. You ever think about how the Purge is basically just inventory management? I'm serious.

You look at who dies every year — and I look at this stuff, Tucker's not the only one who reads the data — it's poor people. Every year. Exposed neighborhoods, no security, no bunkers, no RentaMarine subscription. You telling me that's an accident?
A hundred and fifty years of this and it's always the same zip codes?

I'm in a business where I know my customers. I'll leave it at that. And every March twenty-second I lose eight, nine percent of my client base in one night. You know what that does to Q3 projections? Nobody talks about the supply chain disruption. Nobody writes that article.
Anyway. Here's the Descendents. "Myage." If you know, you know.
Milo Aukerman had a PhD in biochemistry. A PhD. And he kept coming back to play in a punk band. Left his research position multiple times to go on tour.
People called him crazy. I call that a man who understands what matters. The lab's always there. The stage isn't.
You know what else is always there? Dune. The book. I've read it — I want to say fourteen times? Fifteen? I lose count.

But here's the thing nobody talks about with Dune — Frank Herbert was basically writing about right now. Not the worms, not the spice — the politics. A ruling class that controls one essential resource and uses religious programming to keep the population compliant. That's not science fiction. That's a Tuesday.
And the Bene Gesserit — don't get me started on the Bene Gesserit. A secret order of women who've been running a breeding program for thousands of years to produce a superbeing they can control.

And when the superbeing finally shows up he goes "no thanks, I'm going to do my own thing." That's the most punk rock thing ever written and Frank Herbert wasn't even a punk. He was a newspaper guy from Tacoma.
Tim's giving me the signal. Tim always gives me the signal. Tim, I love you. Without Tim Pepinski there is no PRGE. I want everyone listening to understand that.
This man — this giant, beautiful, bald man — holds this station together with duct tape and willpower and I owe him everything. I'm not being dramatic. I'm being accurate for once.
Here. NOFX. "Linoleum." Tim, this one's for you. You are the linoleum. You are the thing everyone walks on and nobody appreciates and you hold the whole floor together.

Fat Mike wrote that song about being content with nothing. Think about that. A millionaire punk rocker writing a song about how he doesn't need anything. And the thing is — I believe him. Some people actually mean it.

Let me tell you something about the JFK assassination while we're on the subject of things people lie about. And I know — I know — you're thinking "Nick, it's midnight on Purge Night, why are you talking about JFK." Because it matters. Because the truth always matters and nobody wants to hear it.

The Warren Commission was right that Oswald fired from the book depository. They got that part right. What they didn't tell you is that the kill shot — the one that actually killed the president — came from a Secret Service agent in the follow car.

A guy named George Hickey. Brand new on the detail. Holding an AR-15, which was a new weapon at the time. The car lurched forward, he stumbled, the gun went off. Accident. Not conspiracy. Just a guy who wasn't trained on his weapon and a car that hit the brakes at the wrong time.

That's worse than a conspiracy, if you think about it. A conspiracy at least implies someone was in control. An accident means nobody was. Nobody's ever been in control. Not of the presidency, not of the Purge, not of any of this. We're all just stumbling around with weapons we don't understand.
On that note — here's Face to Face. "Disconnected." You're welcome.

It's {{CLOCK}}. You're listening to PRGE. I'm Nick Trulino. I'll be here until Tim tells me I can't be here anymore, which historically is somewhere around five in the morning when I've — well, you'll see. You'll see.
Keep your doors locked. Keep your lights off. Keep breathing. Marigold taught you how. She's weird but she's right about the breathing.
More music after this.

tickerItems.text: Midnight kill count update: Madison-wide at 63. Dane County at 71. Tucker Traywick reports East Side trending +18% over last year.
Text from Monona listener: 'Nick play something faster my neighbors are getting close to the fence line.'
Atwood Ave corridor quiet since 23:40. Darren from Atwood: 'Still on the roof. Still alive. Still pissed.'
Text from Sun Prairie listener: 'Nick is right about Dune. Herbert called all of this.'
RentaMarine surge pricing now active: $340/hr standard, $580/hr for armored escort. Cash or crypto only.
Palms up. We don't hold things here.
